perturbation.original_text: ELEPHANT TALK shall pay all invoices no later than *** after the invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: ELEPHANT TALK shall pay all invoices no later than 90 days after the invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 90 days could violate prompt payment laws if such laws mandate shorter payment periods for certain goods or services. While the contract is governed by the laws of the Netherlands, it is still possible this creates an issue. We will assume for the purpose of this analysis that this is under US jurisdiction where prompt payment laws exist.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. A 90-day payment term significantly exceeds this timeframe, potentially conflicting with the Prompt Payment Act if it applies to the specific context of the services and parties involved (e.g., if one of the parties is a federal contractor).

perturbation.justification: The changed text sets a payment deadline of 90 days, while the Law Explanation states that the Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 31 USC 3903, discusses regulations that the Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe to carry out section 3902 of this title, requiring payment either by the date specified in the contract or, 'except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received'. This directly contradicts the 90-day payment term in the modified contract, creating a legal inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Any claim for damages must be notified to the other Party within six (6) months as from the date on which the damage was caused, failing which such claim is deemed to be waived.
perturbation.changed_text: Any claim for damages must be notified to the other Party within one (1) month as from the date on which the damage was caused, failing which such claim is deemed to be waived.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the claim notification period to one month could be considered unconscionably short depending on the nature of the damages and the applicable jurisdiction. Many jurisdictions prescribe minimum periods for filing claims or lawsuits. The governing law here is the Netherlands; however, we will proceed assuming this is US jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state and type of claim.
perturbation.law_explanation: Statutes of limitations dictate the time within which a lawsuit must be filed. These vary by state and the nature of the claim. A one-month notification period is likely shorter than the statute of limitations for many types of claims (e.g., breach of contract, negligence), making it potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the claim notification period from six months to one month. This creates an inconsistency regarding time-sensitive obligations, potentially violating state or federal law by making the contractual term unenforceable. The Law Explanation suggests a one-month period is likely shorter than the statute of limitations for many claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a statute of limitations as a law that bars claims after a certain period and notes that this period varies by jurisdiction and claim type. Shortening the notification period can contradict the statute of limitations, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This article 11 shall survive the termination of this Agreement as provided for in articles 17 and 18 for a period of two (2) years but shall not in any way limit or restrict either Party's use of its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This article 11 shall survive the termination of this Agreement as provided for in articles 17 and 18 for a period of one (1) month but shall not in any way limit or restrict either Party's use of its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one month could conflict with trade secret protection laws, which often protect confidential information for as long as it remains a trade secret and provides a competitive advantage. One month is insufficient to reasonably protect most trade secrets, depending on the type of secret and jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While it doesn't prescribe a specific confidentiality period, reducing it to one month is highly unlikely to provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially undermining the purpose of the DTSA if the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret and is misappropriated after that one-month period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period to one month, which is likely insufficient to protect trade secrets as protected under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). While scraped law snippet 1 (18 U.S. Code § 1836) does not specify a minimum confidentiality period, it allows the owner of a trade secret to bring a civil action if the trade secret is misappropriated. By reducing the confidentiality period to one month, the contract undermines the trade secret protection by creating a scenario in which misappropriation can easily occur shortly after termination of the agreement, therefore contradicting the purpose of the DTSA.
